question: Why does everything keep getting more expensive?

theme.label: Economy and public money
readerQuestions: What is the headline economic number?
Is this a total number or a per-person number?
How much of the story is prices, exchange rates, or GDP share?
What does this explain about household life?

plannedCharts.title: What ₹100 from 2012 buys today
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The most concrete way to feel inflation: the same rupee buys steadily less.

plannedCharts.title: Prices are over 90 times their 1960 level
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The long view that puts a lifetime of inflation in one number.

plannedCharts.title: India's inflation rate, 1960 to today
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Inflation is normal and usually positive; the question is how fast, not whether.

plannedCharts.title: Average inflation, decade by decade
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The cleanest long story, stripped of year-to-year noise.

plannedCharts.title: Inflation month by month since 2012
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The recent, lived experience: the headline rate swings a lot within a few years.

plannedCharts.title: Shops vs mandis: retail (CPI) vs wholesale (WPI)
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Two official inflation measures: what you pay (CPI) vs what producers and traders pay (WPI).

plannedCharts.title: Is India's inflation high? A world comparison
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Whether India's inflation is unusually high — or normal for a fast-growing economy.

plannedCharts.title: What's in the household basket
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Why food dominates how inflation feels: it is nearly half the official basket.

plannedCharts.title: Which categories actually drove the latest inflation
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Weight times speed: a category matters to the headline only if it is both big and rising.

plannedCharts.title: What's getting dearer fastest right now
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The headline hides very different speeds across categories.

plannedCharts.title: How each part of life got pricier since 2012
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Inflation compounds: even 'low' categories pile up over a decade.

plannedCharts.title: Food swings drive the headline
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Because food is half the basket, food prices largely steer the headline.

plannedCharts.title: How food prices climbed, group by group
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Food is not one thing: staples like cereals and milk grind up steadily, others lurch.

plannedCharts.title: The food prices that whipsaw
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: A few items spike and crash hardest — the prices people notice most.

plannedCharts.title: The price of onion, tomato and potato
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The vegetables that make headlines and move household budgets, item by item.

plannedCharts.title: What your groceries cost this year
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The grocery list, item by item — where some prices jumped and others fell.

plannedCharts.title: The quiet compounders: health, education, transport
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Services rarely spike, so they escape attention — but they climb relentlessly.

plannedCharts.title: The price of school fees, doctors and medicine
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The bills families dread, made concrete: school and healthcare.

plannedCharts.title: The price of fuel and power
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Energy at home and on the road — petrol, diesel, the gas cylinder, the power bill.

plannedCharts.title: The biggest price moves this year
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Inflation is an average; the lived experience is the extremes — what spiked and what crashed.

plannedCharts.title: Gold: the household hedge against inflation
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Indians buy gold partly to protect savings from inflation — and it has paid off.

plannedCharts.title: Does your fixed deposit beat inflation?
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The question every saver asks: is my bank deposit actually growing my money?

plannedCharts.title: FD rate vs inflation, the two lines behind the gap
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The real return is just the distance between these two lines.

plannedCharts.title: How the RBI fights inflation: the repo rate
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The main tool against inflation: the central bank's policy rate.

plannedCharts.title: Town and country feel it differently
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: One national number hides that villages and cities buy different baskets.

plannedCharts.title: Food inflation hits the village harder
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Food is a bigger share of a rural household's spend, so food spikes hurt more there.

plannedCharts.title: The wholesale pipeline: where prices start
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Before prices reach you they move through wholesale; this is the pressure upstream.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
